1: The cleansing ritual for healed skin disease (tsara'at) occupies the entire chapter — far more elaborate than the diagnosis laws of ch. 13. The length reflects the ritual's purpose: reintegrating someone who has been socially dead (outside the camp, crying 'Unclean!') back into the covenant community. The cleansing is a form of re-creation and re-admission to covenant life.
2: torat hametsora beyom tohorato: 'the law for the person with skin disease on the day of his cleansing.' The priest does not cleanse the person — YHWH heals; the priest certifies and performs the accompanying ritual. This is a crucial contextual point: the priest examines the healed person outside the camp and confirms the healing. The sequence mirrors the diagnosis of ch. 13 in reverse: what was certified unclean is now certified clean.
3: The priest goes outside the camp to examine — the leper cannot enter the camp until the ritual is complete, so the priest comes to him. The geographic reversal is significant: in ordinary priestly work, people come to the sanctuary; for the leper's cleansing, the priest leaves the sacred center and goes to the margins. This movement pattern will be inverted in the Gospels when Jesus goes to lepers and touches them rather than waiting for them to approach.
4: The four ritual materials: two living clean birds (tziporim ḥayyot tehorot), cedar wood (etz erez), crimson yarn (shni tola'at), and hyssop (ezov). This combination appears also in the red heifer purification (Num 19:6) and in Psalm 51:7 ('purge me with hyssop and I shall be clean'). Cedar was the most precious and durable wood; hyssop the humblest plant that grew from walls (1 Kgs 4:33) — the pairing of greatest to least. Crimson yarn (tola'at shani, lit. worm-crimson) was made from the dye of the scale insect Kermes biblicus; its red color evoked blood.
5: One bird is slaughtered over fresh running water (mayim ḥayyim, lit. living water) in a clay pot. The 'living water' (flowing/spring water, as opposed to cistern water) was required for all major purification rituals in Leviticus and Numbers — it represented uncontaminated, vital water connected to its natural source. The clay pot catches the blood and water mixture used for the sprinkling. The slaughtered bird's blood mingles with the living water.
6: The living bird is dipped — along with the cedar wood, crimson yarn, and hyssop — into the blood-water mixture. The bundle of ritual materials becomes soaked in the purification solution. The living bird absorbs the blood of the dead bird and is then prepared for release. The structural parallel to Yom Kippur's two goats is exact: one animal killed, one released alive — the dead animal's blood is transferred to the living one which carries the purification outward.
7: The sevenfold sprinkling (vehizza al-hamitaher min-hatsara'at sheva pe'amim) on the person being cleansed — the same completion number as in the sin-offering blood rituals. After the sprinkling, the declaration 'he is clean' (vetiher oto) precedes the release of the living bird. The declaration is performative — the priest's words change the person's status. Then the living bird is released into the open field (el-pnei hassadeh), carrying the purification symbolically outward and away, just as the scapegoat carried Israel's sins into the wilderness.
8: Day 1 of the two-stage cleansing: the person washes clothes, shaves all hair, bathes in water, and enters the camp — but does not yet enter his tent (dwelling). The intermediate status is significant: inside the camp boundary but not fully restored to domestic and social life. The shaving of all body hair is an extreme form of renewal — the removal of all that belonged to the period of uncleanness, presenting a new surface to the world.
9: Day 7 protocol: another complete shaving — head, beard, eyebrows, all hair. Then a second laundering and bathing. The repeated shaving creates a visual marker of the cleansing process: the person's changed physical appearance at each stage signals their transitioning ritual status to the community. The seven-day period mirrors other major ritual transitions (ordination, Lev 8:33; post-birth, Lev 12:2).
10: Day 8 brings a full offering complex — the most elaborate in all of Leviticus: two male lambs, one yearling ewe, a grain offering of three-tenths of an ephah of fine flour, and a log of oil (a log = approximately 300 ml). The quantity and variety of offerings reflects the gravity of the reintegration: the person is essentially being re-ordained into covenant life, paralleling the priestly ordination of Lev 8.
11: The cleansed person and all offerings are presented before YHWH at the sanctuary entrance — the formal presentation that marks full covenant standing. The person is now present before YHWH as an offerer, no longer as an outcast. The movement from outside the camp (vv. 2-3) to the sanctuary entrance is the geographic enactment of full reintegration.
12: One male lamb is offered first as a guilt offering (asham) — unusual, since guilt offerings typically address specific property or covenant violations, not ritual impurity. The guilt offering for the leper may reflect the ancient view that prolonged bodily affliction involved some form of covenant guilt (cf. Miriam's leprosy as divine punishment, Num 12). The wave offering (tenufah) before YHWH presents the offering formally for divine acceptance before the blood ritual.
13: The guilt-offering lamb is slaughtered in the holy place (bimqom asher yishḥat et-haḥatta't ve'et-ha'olah bimqom haqqodesh) — the same location as all major offerings. The guilt offering's blood is most holy (qodesh qodashim) — as holy as the sin offering. The spatial consistency of sacrifice ensures that all offerings share the same sacred geography.
14: The blood application to three points: the lobe of the right ear (tenukh ozen yemino), the thumb of the right hand (bohen yad yemino), and the big toe of the right foot (bohen regel yemino). This identical three-point application was used in the priestly ordination ritual (Lev 8:23-24) — blood on Aaron and his sons at the same three points. The leper's cleansing recapitulates ordination: the person is being re-consecrated to covenant life. The three points represent the whole person: hearing (ear), doing (hand), walking (foot) — the re-dedicated life of obedience.
15: Oil from the log of oil is poured into the priest's left palm — creating a pool of oil for the subsequent application. The oil and blood are used in sequence: blood first (vv. 14-17), then oil over the blood (vv. 17-18). The layering of oil over blood connects the cleansing ritual to the broader association of oil with the Spirit and with divine favor. The Spirit (represented by oil in the OT) is poured over the blood-applied person.
16: The sevenfold oil-sprinkling before YHWH — the same completion ritual as the blood-sprinkling but with oil rather than blood. The oil is sprinkled toward the sanctuary rather than on the person — it goes before YHWH as part of the cleansing presentation. This parallels the Yom Kippur blood-sprinkling before the kapporet: something is directed toward the divine presence as part of the purification.
17: Oil is applied to the same three points (ear, thumb, big toe) over the blood already applied there. The oil-over-blood layering creates a composite application at each point. The right side is consistently specified — the right side carried higher status in biblical ritual (the right hand of blessing, the right hand of God in the Psalms). The right ear, right hand, and right foot as the three points of re-dedication cover the whole right side of the person.
18: The remaining oil is poured on the person's head (al-rosh hamitaher). Head anointing was the highest form of consecration in the OT — kings and priests were anointed on the head (1 Sam 10:1; Exod 29:7). The leper's cleansing culminates in a form of anointing, transforming the reintegrated person into someone consecrated to YHWH. The priest makes atonement for the cleansed person before YHWH.
19: The sin offering follows the guilt offering — in the order: guilt offering first (addressing the debt aspect of the disease period), then sin offering (addressing the ritual impurity aspect). The sequence of guilt → sin offering reflects a comprehensive approach: both the guilt dimension and the impurity dimension of the leprosy period require their respective sacrificial responses.
20: The burnt offering (olah) and grain offering (minḥah) conclude the complex — the dedication and tribute offerings that express the person's restored relationship with YHWH. The final atonement formula (vekhipper alav hakohen vetaher) confirms complete cleansing. The full offering sequence (guilt offering → sin offering → burnt offering → grain offering) is the most comprehensive offering complex in Leviticus, reserved for the most dramatic instances of reintegration.
21: The poor person's reduced guilt offering: one lamb instead of two (the minimum viable guilt offering). The graduated-cost provision parallels the burnt-offering scale (ch. 1) and the sin-offering scale (ch. 4-5). The law ensures that economic poverty does not prevent full covenant reintegration — the rite is available at every income level.
22: Two turtledoves or young pigeons replace the sin and burnt offering animals for the poor person. The grain offering is also reduced to one-tenth of an ephah (from three-tenths). These substitutions are functionally equivalent — the same three-point blood and oil application, the same sevenfold sprinkling, the same atoning declaration. The ritual's power lies not in the cost of the materials but in the prescribed procedure.
23: The eighth-day presentation before YHWH applies equally to the poor person — the same day, the same entrance, the same priest. The graduated cost does not alter the timing or the official structure of the cleansing. The offering is brought 'for his cleansing' (letohorato) — the purpose is stated explicitly each time.
24: The guilt-offering lamb and log of oil are waved before YHWH — the same wave offering as the full procedure. The wave offering (tenufah) is maintained even in the reduced version, preserving the essential gesture of formal presentation before YHWH regardless of economic tier.
25: The three-point blood application (ear, thumb, big toe) is performed identically for the poor person's guilt offering — the blood application ritual does not change based on the offering's cost. The reduced offering maintains full ritual integrity at the level of the most sacred acts: the blood and oil applications to the person's body are the same regardless of whether one lamb or two were brought.
26: Oil poured into the priest's left palm — same as the full procedure (v. 15). The economy of the reduced offering is in the animals brought, not in the procedural steps performed. The priestly handling of oil is the same regardless of the offerer's wealth.
27: Sevenfold oil-sprinkling before YHWH — identical to the full procedure. The sevenfold completion ritual is non-negotiable; it cannot be reduced even when the offerings are scaled down for poverty.
28: Oil on the three points over the blood — same as v. 17. The layering of oil over blood is maintained even in the reduced procedure. This consistency ensures that the rich and poor leper receive identical ritual treatment at the level of what matters most — the anointing and the blood application to ear, hand, and foot.
29: Oil poured on the head — the anointing climax. The head anointing is present in the poor person's cleansing as much as in the wealthy person's. The reduction in animal cost does not diminish the dignity and completeness of the restoration. The same formula: atonement before YHWH (lekapper alav lifnei YHWH).
30: One of the two birds is offered — sin or burnt offering. The bird offering for the poor leper parallels the bird offerings available to the poor in other contexts (ch. 1:14-17; Lev 12:8). The bird offering achieves the same cultic goal as the lamb offering; the law's graduated scale never creates a second-class cleansing.
31: The closing formula covers the poor person's offering: the priest makes atonement, and the person is clean. Venislaḥ lo (he shall be forgiven) may appear implicitly here; the cleansing formula (vetaher hakohen et-hamitaher lifnei YHWH) confirms full priestly restoration. The summary law for the poor person's cleansing is given in v. 32.
32: Summary: this is the law for the person with skin disease who cannot afford the standard offerings. The summary formula signals the completion of the person-cleansing legislation before the chapter moves to a new topic: the extension of tsara'at law to houses.
33: The house-disease law is addressed to both Moses and Aaron — the priestly function of inspection and adjudication is required here as in the personal disease laws. The law is explicitly future-oriented: ki tavo'u el-erets Kena'an (when you enter the land of Canaan). The house mold law cannot apply in the wilderness where Israel lives in tents; it is a land-law, anticipating settled life in permanent structures.
34: venatati nega tsara'at bebeit erets aḥuzatkhem: 'I put a mark of skin disease in a house of the land you possess.' The passive agency is startling — YHWH causes the house disease. This is not merely natural mold; it is YHWH's activity in the land. The theological implication: the land itself responds to YHWH's presence and Israel's covenant status. The house disease law will connect to the land-vomiting language of Lev 18:28 — the land is a moral environment.
35: The homeowner reports to the priest — 'something like a disease has appeared in my house.' The subjective reporting (kinegah, 'like a disease') reflects that the homeowner cannot diagnose; only the priest can adjudicate. The reporting mechanism ensures that all disease assessments go through the authorized priestly system — there is no self-diagnosis or self-treatment in Israel's purity law.
36: The priest commands the house to be emptied before inspection — to prevent all household goods from becoming unclean by the priestly declaration. This is a protective measure for the family's property: once the priest declares the house unclean, everything in it becomes unclean. Emptying first preserves household goods. The practical compassion of this ruling reflects the law's care for economic wellbeing alongside ritual concerns.
37: The specific appearance of house disease: greenish or reddish streaks (yeraqqaraqot o adamdammot) that appear deeper than the surface of the walls. This description matches certain types of mold or fungal growth in stone structures. The appearance-based diagnosis (color, depth) parallels the skin-disease diagnosis of ch. 13 — the same diagnostic criteria adapted from human bodies to building structures.
38: The priest leaves the house through the doorway — he does not remain inside — and quarantines (vesagar) the house for seven days. The quarantine mechanism is the same as for human skin disease (ch. 13:4) — the same waiting period before re-examination. The law treats buildings and bodies with the same procedural respect.
39: The seventh-day re-examination: if the marks have spread, the priest proceeds to remediation. The disease spreading in the walls parallels the spreading disease on skin (ch. 13:7-8). The consistent diagnostic criterion of spread vs. non-spread across all disease contexts (human skin, garments, houses) creates a unified theology of purity: that which spreads and corrupts beyond its original boundary is the identifying marker of impurity.
40: Infected stones must be removed and thrown into an unclean place outside the city. The surgical approach — remove the infected material — parallels the scraping and re-plastering of skin-diseased flesh (metaphorically) and the tearing of diseased garments (ch. 13:52). The disposal outside the city maintains the spatial logic of impurity: that which is unclean goes to the margins.
41: The entire interior of the house is scraped and the plaster dumped outside the city in an unclean place. The thorough interior scraping removes the substrate that the mold grew on — it is not sufficient to remove only the obviously infected stones. The remediation addresses the whole interior surface, not just visible marks.
42: New stones and new plaster replace what was removed. The house is restored using entirely new materials — the rebuilt house is fresh, not merely repaired. The restoration parallels the leper's complete shaving and fresh re-presentation: everything associated with the period of disease is removed and replaced.
43: If the disease returns after remediation, it indicates a deeper problem in the house's structure — the mold has penetrated beyond surface remediation. The recurrence criterion parallels the recurrence of skin disease after apparent healing (ch. 13:12-17) — in both cases, persistence of the condition after attempted cleansing signals a more serious underlying issue.
44: The persistent house disease is declared tsara'at mameret — malignant/aggressive disease — and the house is unclean. The diagnosis formula mirrors the human skin disease formula of ch. 13. The priest's declaration is determinative: the house is officially unclean regardless of what the homeowner believes about it.
45: The condemned house is completely demolished: stones, timber, and all plaster must be torn down and carried to an unclean place outside the city. The complete demolition is the harshest possible response — equivalent to the burning of an incurably diseased garment (ch. 13:52). The thoroughness ensures no residual disease material remains in the city.
46: Anyone who enters the quarantined house becomes unclean until evening — the standard time-limited minor impurity. The house transmits impurity by contact just as the diseased person does. The spatial quarantine of the house functions exactly like the personal quarantine of the leper: the contaminated space affects those who enter it.
47: Those who sleep or eat in the quarantined house require clothes-washing — a more involved purification than mere contact-generated impurity. Sleeping and eating are extended intimate contacts that produce greater contamination than a brief visit. The graduated response (sleep/eat → clothes washing; enter briefly → unclean until evening) is consistent with the graduated contact-severity principle throughout Leviticus 15.
48: If the re-plastered house shows no disease recurrence, the priest declares it clean — the disease did not spread after remediation. The declaration of cleanliness is as determinative as the declaration of uncleanness: the priest's word changes the legal and ritual status of the house officially.
49: The house cleansing ritual uses the same four materials as the personal cleansing (two birds, cedar wood, crimson yarn, hyssop). The structural parallel between person-cleansing and house-cleansing is deliberate: the same purification technology applies to both. The house can be symbolically cleansed just as the person can — with blood, water, and the release of the living bird.
50: One bird slaughtered over fresh water in a clay pot — exactly as in v. 5 for the personal cleansing. The consistency of the two-bird ritual across personal and house cleansing contexts demonstrates its status as a fixed purification protocol for tsara'at specifically, distinct from other purification rituals.
51: The bundle of materials (cedar, hyssop, crimson yarn, living bird) is dipped in the blood-water and used to sprinkle the house seven times. The house receives the same sevenfold sprinkling as the person (v. 7) — the completion number is maintained. The living bird is used to carry the purification solution into the house's interior, reaching every part of the structure.
52: The house is purified by the blood of the bird, the fresh water, the living bird, the cedar wood, the hyssop, and the crimson yarn. The comprehensive list of purifying agents reflects a multi-modal purification: blood (life), water (cleansing), the living bird (vitality), cedar (durability), hyssop (humility), crimson (life-color). Each element contributes something to the purification's symbolic completeness.
53: The living bird is released outside the city into the open field — the same release as in v. 7, but now outside the city rather than outside the camp. The release carries the purification symbolically away from the healed space. The atonement formula (vekhipper al-habayit vetaher) applies to a building: YHWH's atonement can cover structures as well as persons.
54: Summary heading for the entire tsara'at legislation (chs. 13-14): every kind of skin disease, every itch (neteg). The summary runs through v. 57 and covers all types addressed in both chapters.
55: The garment-disease (ch. 13:47-59) and house-disease (ch. 14:33-53) are both explicitly included in the summary. The three domains of tsara'at — person, garment, house — form a coherent system covering all the spaces a human being inhabits: body, clothing, home.
56: The three presentations of disease on the body: swelling (se'et), scab (sappaḥat), and bright spot (baheret) — the diagnostic triad of ch. 13:2. The summary restates the diagnostic categories, tying the complex diagnostic and cleansing legislation into a unified legal corpus.
57: lehorot beyom hatame uveyom hatahor: 'to teach when something is unclean and when it is clean.' The purpose of the entire tsara'at legislation — and by extension, of Israel's purity system generally — is instruction in discernment: the capacity to distinguish clean from unclean, the holy from the common. This educational purpose connects the purity laws to the creation theology of Gen 1 (where YHWH distinguishes light/dark, water/land) and to the Levitical priestly vocation of lehabdil (to distinguish/separate, Lev 10:10). The law teaches through its procedures.
